title: CPR BLS Sequence
subtitle: Drag the steps into the correct AHA BLS order before seeing the reveal.

cases.prompt: Adult found unresponsive and not breathing normally. You are the single rescuer on scene. No AED is immediately available — one is being retrieved by a bystander.
cases.hint: Recognize → activate → compress. The AED arrives while you are working — integrate it without stopping compressions until analysis requires it.

steps.label: Confirm unresponsive and absent or abnormal breathing — shout for help and call 9-1-1
Check carotid pulse no more than 10 seconds
Begin 30:2 CPR at 100–120 compressions per minute
Bystander arrives with AED — apply pads without interrupting compressions
Pause compressions for AED rhythm analysis when device prompts
Shock if advised, then immediately resume CPR; resume CPR without shock if no shock is indicated

cases.explanation: Single-rescuer adult BLS prioritizes recognition, activation, and immediate CPR. When no AED is at hand, compressions start first — do not wait. Pad application happens in parallel once the device arrives, but motion stops only for the brief analysis window. Immediate resumption after shock or no-shock decision is non-negotiable.

cases.prompt: Two-month-old infant found unresponsive and apneic. Two rescuers are present. An AED with pediatric pads is available.
cases.hint: Infant pulse check is brachial, not carotid. Two-rescuer infant technique uses two thumbs encircling the chest. The C:V ratio changes with two rescuers.

steps.label: Confirm unresponsive and absent or abnormal breathing — activate EMS
Check brachial pulse no more than 10 seconds
Rescuer 1 begins two-thumb-encircling chest compressions at 15:2, depth at least 1.5 in (4 cm)
Rescuer 2 applies AED with pediatric pads while compressions continue
Pause compressions for AED analysis — shock if advised, then immediately resume 15:2 CPR

cases.explanation: Infant cardiac arrest most commonly has a respiratory origin, making effective ventilation critical. The brachial artery is the correct pulse check site in infants. Two-thumb-encircling technique generates superior compression depth and coronary perfusion pressure compared to two-finger technique. Two-rescuer infant CPR uses 15:2 — not 30:2 — before advanced airway placement. Minimum compression depth is 1.5 in (4 cm), or at least one-third the AP chest diameter.

cases.prompt: EMS crew arrives to find a bystander performing single-rescuer CPR on an adult. Compressions are in progress. Your team has a monitor/AED.
cases.hint: Do not stop compressions to take over — integrate seamlessly. Assign roles before the next analysis cycle. The first shock decision comes after a complete two-minute cycle.

steps.label: Seamlessly assume compressions without interrupting the current cycle — confirm scene safety and patient status
Team leader assigns roles: compressor, airway/ventilation, AED/monitor operator
Apply monitor/defibrillator pads while compressions continue — do not pause
Complete two-minute CPR cycle at 100–120 compressions per minute with 30:2 ratio
Pause compressions for AED rhythm analysis — minimize hands-off time
Shock if advised and immediately resume CPR; if no shock, immediately resume CPR and reassess rhythm after next two-minute cycle

cases.explanation: EMS team arrival is a high-risk interruption point. Seamless takeover without pausing compressions is the standard. Role assignment happens concurrently with ongoing CPR, not as a reason to pause it. Pad application during compressions keeps the first analysis on schedule. The AHA targets a chest compression fraction greater than 80% — each unnecessary pause erodes that metric. Analysis pauses should not exceed 10 seconds.
